question:
Let $A, B, C$ denote distinct points with integer coordinates in $\mathbb
R^2$.  Prove that if
\[(|AB|+|BC|)^2<8\cdot [ABC]+1\]
then $A, B, C$ are three vertices of a square.  Here $|XY|$ is the length
of segment $XY$ and $[ABC]$ is the area of triangle $ABC$.
solution:
Recall the inequalities $|AB|^2 + |BC|^2 \geq 2|AB||BC|$ (AM-GM)
and $|AB||BC| \geq 2[ABC]$ (Law of Sines). Also recall that the area of
a triangle with integer coordinates is half an integer
(if its vertices lie at $(0,0), (p,q), (r,s)$, the area is
$|ps-qr|/2$), and that if $A$ and $B$ have integer coordinates, then
$|AB|^2$
is an integer (Pythagoras). Now observe that
\begin{align*}
8[ABC] &\leq |AB|^2+|BC|^2 + 4[ABC] \\
&\leq |AB|^2 + |BC|^2 + 2|AB| |BC| \\
&< 8[ABC]+1,
\end{align*}
and that the first and second expressions are both integers.
We conclude that
$8[ABC] = |AB|^2+ |BC|^2+4[ABC]$, and so $|AB|^2+|BC|^2 =
2|AB| |BC|
= 4[ABC]$; that is, $B$ is a right angle and $AB=BC$, as desired.

descriptive_long.question:
Let $vertexa, vertexb, vertexc$ denote distinct points with integer coordinates in $\mathbb R^2$.  Prove that if
\[(|vertexavertexb|+|vertexbvertexc|)^2<8\cdot [vertexavertexbvertexc]+1\]
then $vertexa, vertexb, vertexc$ are three vertices of a square.  Here $|vertexxvertexy|$ is the length
of segment $vertexxvertexy$ and $[vertexavertexbvertexc]$ is the area of triangle $vertexavertexbvertexc$.
descriptive_long.solution:
Recall the inequalities $|vertexavertexb|^2 + |vertexbvertexc|^2 \geq 2|vertexavertexb||vertexbvertexc|$ (AM-GM)
and $|vertexavertexb||vertexbvertexc| \geq 2[vertexavertexbvertexc]$ (Law of Sines). Also recall that the area of
a triangle with integer coordinates is half an integer
(if its vertices lie at $(0,0), (coordp,coordq), (coordr,coords)$, the area is
$|coordpcoords-coordqcoordr|/2$), and that if $vertexa$ and $vertexb$ have integer coordinates, then
$|vertexavertexb|^2$
is an integer (Pythagoras). Now observe that
\begin{align*}
8[vertexavertexbvertexc] &\leq |vertexavertexb|^2+|vertexbvertexc|^2 + 4[vertexavertexbvertexc] \\
&\leq |vertexavertexb|^2 + |vertexbvertexc|^2 + 2|vertexavertexb| |vertexbvertexc| \\
&< 8[vertexavertexbvertexc]+1,
\end{align*}
and that the first and second expressions are both integers.
We conclude that
$8[vertexavertexbvertexc] = |vertexavertexb|^2+ |vertexbvertexc|^2+4[vertexavertexbvertexc]$, and so $|vertexavertexb|^2+|vertexbvertexc|^2 =
2|vertexavertexb| |vertexbvertexc|
= 4[vertexavertexbvertexc]$; that is, $vertexb$ is a right angle and $vertexavertexb=vertexbvertexc$, as desired.

descriptive_long_confusing.question:
Let $dandelion, peppermint, chandelier$ denote distinct points with integer coordinates in $\mathbb R^2$.  Prove that if
\[(|dandelionpeppermint|+|peppermintchandelier|)^2<8\cdot [dandelionpeppermintchandelier]+1\]
then $dandelion, peppermint, chandelier$ are three vertices of a square.  Here $|accordionbrainstorm|$ is the length
of segment $accordionbrainstorm$ and $[dandelionpeppermintchandelier]$ is the area of triangle $dandelionpeppermintchandelier$.
descriptive_long_confusing.solution:
Recall the inequalities $|dandelionpeppermint|^2 + |peppermintchandelier|^2 \geq 2|dandelionpeppermint||peppermintchandelier|$ (AM-GM)
and $|dandelionpeppermint||peppermintchandelier| \geq 2[dandelionpeppermintchandelier]$ (Law of Sines). Also recall that the area of
a triangle with integer coordinates is half an integer
(if its vertices lie at $(0,0), (rainforest,lighthouse), (farmhouse,chocolate)$, the area is
$|rainforest chocolate - farmhouse lighthouse|/2$), and that if $dandelion$ and $peppermint$ have integer coordinates, then
$|dandelionpeppermint|^2$
is an integer (Pythagoras). Now observe that
\begin{align*}
8[dandelionpeppermintchandelier] &\leq |dandelionpeppermint|^2+|peppermintchandelier|^2 + 4[dandelionpeppermintchandelier] \\
&\leq |dandelionpeppermint|^2 + |peppermintchandelier|^2 + 2|dandelionpeppermint| |peppermintchandelier| \\
&< 8[dandelionpeppermintchandelier]+1,
\end{align*}
and that the first and second expressions are both integers.
We conclude that
$8[dandelionpeppermintchandelier] = |dandelionpeppermint|^2+ |peppermintchandelier|^2+4[dandelionpeppermintchandelier]$, and so $|dandelionpeppermint|^2+|peppermintchandelier|^2 =
2|dandelionpeppermint| |peppermintchandelier|
= 4[dandelionpeppermintchandelier]$; that is, $peppermint$ is a right angle and $dandelionpeppermint = peppermintchandelier$, as desired.

descriptive_long_misleading.question:
Let $nonlocality, wholeness, everywhere$ denote distinct points with integer coordinates in $\mathbb R^2$.  Prove that if
\[(|nonlocalitywholeness|+|wholenesseverywhere|)^2<8\cdot [nonlocalitywholenesseverywhere]+1\]
then $nonlocality, wholeness, everywhere$ are three vertices of a square.  Here $|nothingnessemptiness|$ is the length
of segment $nothingnessemptiness$ and $[nonlocalitywholenesseverywhere]$ is the area of triangle $nonlocalitywholenesseverywhere$.
descriptive_long_misleading.solution:
Recall the inequalities $|nonlocalitywholeness|^2 + |wholenesseverywhere|^2 \geq 2|nonlocalitywholeness||wholenesseverywhere|$ (AM-GM)
and $|nonlocalitywholeness||wholenesseverywhere| \geq 2[nonlocalitywholenesseverywhere]$ (Law of Sines). Also recall that the area of
a triangle with integer coordinates is half an integer
(if its vertices lie at $(0,0), (voidness,limitless), (vastness,endlessness)$, the area is
$|voidnessendlessness-limitlessvastness|/2$), and that if $nonlocality$ and $wholeness$ have integer coordinates, then
$|nonlocalitywholeness|^2$
is an integer (Pythagoras). Now observe that
\begin{align*}
8[nonlocalitywholenesseverywhere] &\leq |nonlocalitywholeness|^2+|wholenesseverywhere|^2 + 4[nonlocalitywholenesseverywhere] \\
&\leq |nonlocalitywholeness|^2 + |wholenesseverywhere|^2 + 2|nonlocalitywholeness| |wholenesseverywhere| \\
&< 8[nonlocalitywholenesseverywhere]+1,
\end{align*}
and that the first and second expressions are both integers.
We conclude that
$8[nonlocalitywholenesseverywhere] = |nonlocalitywholeness|^2+ |wholenesseverywhere|^2+4[nonlocalitywholenesseverywhere]$, and so $|nonlocalitywholeness|^2+|wholenesseverywhere|^2 =
2|nonlocalitywholeness| |wholenesseverywhere|
= 4[nonlocalitywholenesseverywhere]$; that is, $wholeness$ is a right angle and $nonlocalitywholeness=wholenesseverywhere$, as desired.

garbled_string.question:
Let $qzxwvtnp, hjgrksla, mnbvcxqe$ denote distinct points with integer coordinates in $\mathbb
R^2$.  Prove that if
\[(|qzxwvtnphjgrksla|+|hjgrkslamnbvcxqe|)^2<8\cdot [qzxwvtnphjgrkslamnbvcxqe]+1\]
then $qzxwvtnp, hjgrksla, mnbvcxqe$ are three vertices of a square.  Here $|plokijuhedcrfvtg|$ is the length
of segment $plokijuhedcrfvtg$ and $[qzxwvtnphjgrkslamnbvcxqe]$ is the area of triangle $qzxwvtnphjgrkslamnbvcxqe$.
garbled_string.solution:
Recall the inequalities $|qzxwvtnphjgrksla|^2 + |hjgrkslamnbvcxqe|^2 \geq 2|qzxwvtnphjgrksla||hjgrkslamnbvcxqe|$ (AM-GM)
and $|qzxwvtnphjgrksla||hjgrkslamnbvcxqe| \geq 2[qzxwvtnphjgrkslamnbvcxqe]$ (Law of Sines). Also recall that the area of
a triangle with integer coordinates is half an integer
(if its vertices lie at $(0,0), (qazmlpoh,wsxneirg), (edcvfjkl,rfvtgnhy)$, the area is
$|qazmlpohrfvtgnhy-wsxneirgedcvfjkl|/2$), and that if $qzxwvtnp$ and $hjgrksla$ have integer coordinates, then
$|qzxwvtnphjgrksla|^2$
is an integer (Pythagoras). Now observe that
\begin{align*}
8[qzxwvtnphjgrkslamnbvcxqe] &\leq |qzxwvtnphjgrksla|^2+|hjgrkslamnbvcxqe|^2 + 4[qzxwvtnphjgrkslamnbvcxqe] \\
&\leq |qzxwvtnphjgrksla|^2 + |hjgrkslamnbvcxqe|^2 + 2|qzxwvtnphjgrksla| |hjgrkslamnbvcxqe| \\
&< 8[qzxwvtnphjgrkslamnbvcxqe]+1,
\end{align*}
and that the first and second expressions are both integers.
We conclude that
$8[qzxwvtnphjgrkslamnbvcxqe] = |qzxwvtnphjgrksla|^2+ |hjgrkslamnbvcxqe|^2+4[qzxwvtnphjgrkslamnbvcxqe]$, and so $|qzxwvtnphjgrksla|^2+|hjgrkslamnbvcxqe|^2 =
2|qzxwvtnphjgrksla| |hjgrkslamnbvcxqe|
= 4[qzxwvtnphjgrkslamnbvcxqe]$; that is, $hjgrksla$ is a right angle and $qzxwvtnphjgrksla=hjgrkslamnbvcxqe$, as desired.

kernel_variant.question:
Let $A,B,C$ be distinct points with integer coordinates in the plane.  Assume that the two sides $BC$ and $CA$ of triangle $ABC$ satisfy

\[
 igl(|BC|+|CA|\bigr)^2 \\;<\\; 8\,[ABC] \\+\\ \tfrac12,
\]

where $|XY|$ denotes the Euclidean distance between $X$ and $Y$, and $[ABC]$ denotes the area of $\triangle ABC$.  Prove that under this hypothesis the points $A,B,C$ are three consecutive vertices of a square.

(For comparison, the more familiar inequality
\[(|AB|+|BC|)^2<8\,[ABC]+1\]
leads to the same conclusion; the present formulation is an equivalent---indeed slightly stronger---variant obtained by relabelling the vertices and rescaling the additive constant.)
kernel_variant.solution:
Throughout, let
  a = |BC|,\; b = |CA|,\quad\text{and}\quad 2[ABC]=ab\sin C,           \tag{1}
where $C=\angle BCA$.

Step 1 (Lattice facts).  If two lattice points $P,Q$ are given then $|PQ|^2\in\mathbb Z$ (Pythagoras).  For any lattice triangle the doubled area $2[ABC]$ is an integer (Pick/Shoelace formula).

Step 2 (A chain of inequalities).
Using $(1)$ we have $ab\ge 2[ABC]$.  Hence
\[
(a+b)^2=a^2+b^2+2ab\ge a^2+b^2+4[ABC].               \tag{2}
\]
On the other hand the AM-GM inequality gives $a^2+b^2\ge 2ab\ge 4[ABC]$, so
\[
a^2+b^2+4[ABC]\ge 8[ABC].                               \tag{3}
\]

Step 3 (An integer squeeze).
Set
\[
N=a^2+b^2+4[ABC], \qquad M=8[ABC].
\]
Because $a^2,b^2\in\mathbb Z$ and $2[ABC]\in\mathbb Z$, both $N$ and $M$ are integers.  From (2) and (3) we have
\[
M\le N\le(a+b)^2< M+\tfrac12.                            \tag{4}
\]
No integer lies strictly between $M$ and $M+\tfrac12$, so (4) forces $N=M$.  Consequently
\[
a^2+b^2=4[ABC].                                         \tag{5}
\]

Step 4 (The equality conditions).
Equality in (2) requires $ab=2[ABC]$, i.e. $\sin C=1$ and $\angle C=90^\circ$.  Equality in the AM-GM step demands $a=b$.  Hence $\triangle ABC$ is right-isosceles with right angle at $C$ and legs $BC=CA$.

Step 5 (Geometric conclusion).
Since $BC\perp CA$ and $BC=CA$, the segments $BC$ and $CA$ are adjacent, congruent, perpendicular edges of a square.  Therefore the points $B,C,A$ (in that order) are three consecutive vertices of this square, completing the proof.

core_steps: Lattice fact: for integer-coordinate points, |AB|² and 2·[ABC] are integers.
Lower bound via AM–GM and Law of Sines: (|AB|+|BC|)² ≥ |AB|²+|BC|²+4[ABC] ≥ 8·[ABC].
Integer squeeze: hypothesis gives (|AB|+|BC|)² < 8·[ABC]+1, so the integer |AB|²+|BC|²+4[ABC] must equal 8·[ABC].
Equality cases force AB = BC and ∠B = 90° (right-isosceles triangle).
Such a triangle matches three consecutive vertices of a square.

slot1.description: Additive margin that must be <1 so no integer fits strictly between the bounds.

slot2.description: Choice of the two consecutive sides whose lengths are summed; any cyclic permutation of the vertices works.
